sanskrit_devanagari:
न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्न‍ुते ।
न च सन्न्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति ॥ ४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na karmaṇām anārambhān naiṣkarmyaṁ puruṣo 'śnute |
na ca sannyasanād eva siddhiṁ samadhigacchati || 4 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Not by merely abstaining from work can one achieve freedom from reaction, nor by renunciation alone can one attain perfection.
Not by merely abstaining from work can one achieve freedom from reaction, nor by renunciation alone can one attain perfection.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 931 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The renounced order of life can be accepted when one has been purified by the discharge of the prescribed form of duties which are laid down just to purify the hearts of materialistic men. Without purification, one cannot attain success by abruptly adopting the fourth order of life (sannyāsa).

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.4 Purusah, a person; na does not; asnute, attain; naiskarmyam, freedom from action, the state of being free from action, steadfastness in the Yoga of Knowledge, i.e. the state of abiding in one’s own Self which is free from action; anarambhat, by abstaining; karmanam, from actions-by the non-perfo…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-4-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading is sharp: naiṣkarmya = the state of abiding in one's own Self which is free from action; this state is NOT attained by anārambhāt — by mere non-undertaking — of sacrifices and prescribed action. Prescribed action purifies the mind by attenuating sins; without that purification, jñāna-niṣṭhā cannot arise. Renunciation alone (sannyasanād eva) without prior purification yields nothing. The verse rejects the apparently quickest route — just stop — as actually the slowest, because it skips the prerequisite.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.4 Not by non-performance of the acts prescribed by the scriptures, does a person attain freedom from Karma, i.e., Jnana Yoga; nor by ceasing to perform such actions as are prescribed in the scriptures and are already begun by him.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-4-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: 'Not by non-performance of the acts prescribed by the scriptures does a person attain freedom from Karma (i.e., Jñāna Yoga); nor by ceasing to perform such actions...' Success comes through actions done WITHOUT ATTACHMENT TO THE FRUITS, performed AS WORSHIP OF THE SUPREME PERSON. Without that orientation, jñāna-niṣṭhā cannot arise. The verse rules out abstention as a strategy and replaces it with karma-yoga-as-worship.

positions.rendering: freedom from karmic reactions / mere physical renunciation
naiṣkarmya = state of abiding in one's Self which is free from action; not attained by non-performance of sacrifices and other prescribed actions
naiṣkarmya = jñāna-yoga; not attained by non-performance of scriptural acts; success comes through actions done without attachment to fruits, by way of worshipping the Supreme Person

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: naiṣkarmya is the state of being free from action's binding. Both ācāryas agree: the state cannot be reached by abstention. The Advaita reading takes naiṣkarmya as Self-abidance, with karma-yoga as the purificatory pre-stage. The Vishishtadvaita reading takes naiṣkarmya as jñāna-niṣṭhā arising from karma-yoga performed as worship. The doctrinal disagreement is about WHAT karma-yoga's role is (preparation vs. own-mode of worship), but both reject the strategy of 'don't act, become free.' Modern translators preserve the rejection but flatten the underlying disagreement.

literal_meaning: Not by non-undertaking of action does a person attain naiṣkarmya (freedom from action), nor by mere renunciation does one attain perfection.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.4 begins the famous dual claim. naiṣkarmya — the state of being free from action — is NOT attained by non-undertaking (anārambhāt); siddhi (perfection) is NOT attained by mere renunciation (sannyasanād eva). Both ācāryas read this as the foundational rejection of the questioner's strategy of laying down arms. Per the Advaita reading: the karma-yogin purifies the mind through action and only then becomes eligible for jñāna-niṣṭhā; abstaining is not a shortcut. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: success comes from acts done without attachment, performed as worship of the Supreme Person; without these, jñāna-niṣṭhā cannot arise. Doctrinally pivotal: the renunciate path is real, but you do not enter it by quitting.
